embedding_text: Staffordshire Bull Terrier. Medium terrier, 24-38 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 10. Very allergy-prone. Prone to: skin allergies (dominant concern), L2HGA (breed-specific metabolic disorder — seizures, wobbliness), cataracts, mast cell tumors. Stoic — hides pain. Extremely people-oriented. Common meds: Apoquel/Cytopoint, limited ingredient diets. Red flags: seizures/wobbliness (L2HGA), skin lumps (mast cell), chronic skin issues, personality withdrawal.

health_predispositions.description: Pendulous ears trap moisture — chronic ear infections very common
Common in medium sporting breeds
Multiple inherited eye diseases
Breed-specific metabolic disorder affecting red blood cells and muscles
Some predisposition to immune-mediated diseases

behavioral_traits.temperament: Friendly, eager, energetic. Classic bird dog — loves fieldwork.
behavioral_traits.exercise_needs: High — needs vigorous daily exercise and mental stimulation.
behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Generally happy. Can be hyperactive without exercise.
notable_behaviors: 'Springer rage' — very rare sudden aggression episodes in some lines (genetic, not behavioral)
High energy — behavioral changes are quickly noticeable
Loves water — ear care after swimming is critical
Strong retrieving drive — mouth injuries possible from fetching

age_specific_concerns.puppy_0_to_1: Eye screening. Ear care routine. PFK testing available.

age_specific_concerns.adult_3_to_7: Eye exams. Ear maintenance. Exercise and joint balance.

breed_specific_red_flags: Dark urine after exercise (PFK deficiency — breed-specific)
Eye pain or sudden vision loss (glaucoma — can cause rapid blindness)
Sudden unprovoked aggression ('Springer rage' — rare but breed-specific)
Chronic ear infections not responding to treatment
embedding_text: English Springer Spaniel. Medium sporting breed, 40-50 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 10. Prone to: ear infections (pendulous ears), hip dysplasia, eye diseases (PRA, glaucoma), PFK deficiency (dark urine after exercise — breed-specific), autoimmune conditions. High energy — loves water. 'Springer rage' is rare but breed-specific. Common meds: ear cleaners, eye medications, NSAIDs. Red flags: dark urine after exercise (PFK), eye pain (glaucoma), sudden unprovoked aggression, chronic ear infections.

health_predispositions.description: Common in large breeds
Deep-chested breed at bloat risk
Eyelid rolling inward causing irritation
Some individual sensitivity reported in the breed

behavioral_traits.temperament: Independent, protective, calm. Livestock guardian breed — makes independent decisions.
behavioral_traits.exercise_needs: Moderate — needs space but not intense structured exercise.
behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Independent. Can be aloof with strangers. May be anxious in confined spaces.
notable_behaviors: Livestock guardian — independent thinker, not a people-pleaser like most breeds
Nocturnal barking is guarding instinct, NOT a behavioral problem
Thick white double coat = heat sensitive in warm climates
May be stressed by confinement or small spaces — bred for open territory

age_specific_concerns.adult_3_to_7: Bloat prevention. Joint monitoring. Eye health.
age_specific_concerns.senior_7_plus: Joint support. Cancer screening. Quality of life assessment.

Dramatic personality change in normally calm dog (investigate pain or illness)
Eye irritation or squinting (entropion)
Panting or distress in warm weather (heat sensitivity)

common_medications.notes: Surgery may be needed for severe cases.

embedding_text: Maremma Sheepdog. Large guardian breed, 65-100 lbs, life expectancy 11-13 years, senior at 8. Livestock guardian — independent, not people-pleaser. Prone to: hip dysplasia, bloat/GDV, entropion (eye condition), anesthesia sensitivity. Nocturnal barking is normal guardian behavior, not a problem. Heat sensitive (thick white coat). May be stressed by confinement. Common meds: joint supplements, NSAIDs, eye ointments. Red flags: bloat symptoms, dramatic personality change, eye irritation, heat distress.

Degenerative valve disease common in aging small dogs
Weakened tracheal rings common in very small dogs
Dogs under 10 lbs are at higher risk for blood sugar drops

behavioral_traits.temperament: Variable — mixed breeds have diverse temperaments based on genetic background.

age_specific_concerns.adult_3_to_7: Annual dental assessment. Heart murmur screening. Patellar check.
age_specific_concerns.senior_7_plus: Heart disease risk increases. Dental disease progression. Joint support. Cognitive decline.

Coughing at night (heart disease)

embedding_text: Mixed Breed Small Dog (under 25 lbs). Life expectancy 12-16 years, senior at 11. Without known breed composition, use size-based health framework. Small mixed dogs are commonly prone to: dental disease (crowded teeth), patellar luxation, heart disease (MVD in seniors), collapsed trachea (very small dogs), hypoglycemia (under 10 lbs). Mixed breed health is unpredictable — cannot assume breed-specific risks or protections. Small size means illness escalates faster. Common meds: dental care, heart medications, joint supplements. Red flags: not eating 12+ hours if very small, honking cough, nighttime coughing, persistent limping.

Active medium dogs are at risk, especially if overweight

Especially if floppy-eared
Common when activity level doesn't match food intake

Chronic ear infections if floppy-eared
Any symptom that doesn't resolve within expected timeframe (no breed-specific prediction available — err toward vet evaluation)

embedding_text: Mixed Breed Medium Dog (25-55 lbs). Life expectancy 10-14 years, senior at 9. Without known breed composition, use size and physical traits as health framework. Medium mixed dogs are commonly prone to: hip dysplasia, cruciate tears (active dogs), allergies, ear infections (if floppy-eared), obesity. Assess body shape for additional risks: deep chest = bloat awareness, long back = spine caution, floppy ears = ear care. Rescue dogs may have baseline parasite and dental neglect. Common meds: NSAIDs, allergy medications, ear cleaners. Red flags: sudden hind lameness, bloat symptoms if deep-chested, chronic ear infections, symptoms not resolving as expected.

Heavy body weight stresses knee ligaments
Bone cancer risk increases with body size

Sudden hind leg lameness (cruciate tear)
Dramatic behavioral change (investigate pain — large dogs may mask discomfort)

common_medications.notes: Very common need in large dogs. Monitor for GI side effects.
Start from young adulthood for large dogs.
Consider during spay/neuter if body shape suggests bloat risk.

embedding_text: Mixed Breed Large Dog (55+ lbs). Life expectancy 8-13 years, senior at 8. Without known breed composition, use size and body shape as health framework. Large mixed dogs are commonly prone to: hip/elbow dysplasia, bloat/GDV (if deep-chested), cruciate tears, osteosarcoma (risk increases with size), arthritis. Assess body shape: deep/narrow chest = bloat risk, heavy build = joint stress, long back = spine caution. Weight management critical — every extra pound impacts joints. Common meds: NSAIDs, joint supplements, gastropexy if deep-chested. Red flags: bloat symptoms (if deep-chested), persistent single-limb limping (bone cancer), sudden hind lameness, dramatic behavioral change.

health_predispositions.description: Very common in cats, especially indoor, overweight males. Male urinary blockage is life-threatening.
common_signs: frequent visits to litter box with little output

health_predispositions.description: Indoor cats are highly prone to weight gain, increasing diabetes and joint disease risk
Very common and often under-diagnosed in cats
One of the most common senior cat conditions
Most common endocrine disease in older cats
Common in overweight indoor cats

behavioral_traits.temperament: Highly variable — as diverse as their genetics.
behavioral_traits.exercise_needs: Moderate — indoor enrichment important for indoor-only cats.
behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Variable. Cats in general hide stress and illness well.
notable_behaviors: Cats mask illness exceptionally well — subtle changes (hiding, reduced grooming, litter box changes) are often the FIRST and ONLY signs
Indoor-only cats have different risk profiles than indoor-outdoor cats
Overweight indoor male cats are the highest-risk demographic for urinary blockage
Litter box changes are often the earliest sign of medical issues in cats
Not eating for 24+ hours is dangerous for cats (risk of liver failure from fat mobilization)

age_specific_concerns.kitten_0_to_1: Vaccination schedule. Spay/neuter timing. Parasite prevention. URI susceptibility.
age_specific_concerns.young_adult_1_to_6: Weight management (especially indoor cats). Dental care. Behavioral enrichment. Urinary health.
age_specific_concerns.adult_7_to_10: Dental disease progression. Baseline bloodwork recommended. Weight monitoring. Diabetes risk if overweight.
age_specific_concerns.senior_11_plus: Kidney disease screening (bloodwork + urinalysis). Thyroid screening. Blood pressure. Dental management. Cancer awareness.

breed_specific_red_flags: Male cat straining to urinate or not producing urine (URINARY BLOCKAGE — life-threatening emergency, fatal within 24-48 hours)
Not eating for 24+ hours (cats cannot safely fast — liver failure risk)
Hiding for 24+ hours with no interaction (significant illness signal)
Open-mouth breathing or panting (ALWAYS an emergency in cats unlike dogs)
Weight loss despite normal or increased appetite in a senior cat (thyroid, diabetes, or kidney disease)
Sudden hind leg paralysis or cold hind legs (blood clot — often from undiagnosed heart disease)

common_medications.medication: Urinary prescription diet (Royal Canin SO, Hill's c/d)
common_medications.used_for: Urinary tract health and stone prevention
common_medications.notes: Common for cats with FLUTD history. Lifelong diet change.
Daily medication. Requires periodic blood monitoring.
Owners often learn to administer at home for CKD cats.
Twice daily injections. Some cats achieve remission with early treatment and diet change.

common_medications.used_for: Pain management and vet visit anxiety
common_medications.notes: Very commonly given before vet visits to reduce stress. Also used for chronic pain.

embedding_text: Mixed Breed Cat / Domestic Cat (DSH/DLH/DMH). Typically 6-15 lbs, life expectancy 12-20 years, senior at 11. Not a true breed — use weight, age, sex, and lifestyle (indoor/outdoor) as primary health framework. Overweight indoor male cats are HIGHEST risk for urinary blockage (emergency — fatal within 24-48 hours). Common conditions: FLUTD/urinary issues, obesity, dental disease, kidney disease (senior), hyperthyroidism (senior), diabetes (overweight cats). Cats CANNOT safely fast — not eating 24+ hours risks liver failure. Open-mouth breathing is ALWAYS an emergency in cats. Cats mask illness — litter box changes, hiding, reduced grooming are key early signals. Common meds: urinary diet, methimazole for thyroid, subQ fluids for kidneys, insulin for diabetes, gabapentin for pain/anxiety. Red flags: male straining to urinate (emergency), not eating 24+ hours, hiding 24+ hours, open-mouth breathing, weight loss despite eating, hind leg paralysis.
